subject: Astronomy & Space
title: The Observable Universe
description: A fast breakdown of The Observable Universe for builders.

hook.text: We can only see 46.5 billion light-years in any direction. Beyond that is the unobservable universe.

buildup.text: Light has had 13.8 billion years to reach us, but expansion has stretched space—so the edge is much farther out.

discovery.text: The observable universe contains roughly 2 trillion galaxies and 10²⁴ stars. And that's just what we can see.

twist.text: The full universe may be vastly larger—or even infinite. We may never know what lies beyond our cosmic horizon.

climax.text: As the universe expands, distant galaxies are being pushed beyond our view. The observable universe is shrinking.

punchline.text: Two trillion galaxies we can see. How many more lie beyond the horizon?

quiz.question: Why is the observable universe larger than 13.8 billion light-years in radius?
options: Because the expansion of space has stretched distances beyond the age of light travel
Because light traveled faster in the early universe
Because multiple Big Bangs created overlapping observable regions
